turns.text: Hello Agnes, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels... strange to talk about it out loud, I think. Most days, I just keep it tucked away inside, like I’m holding my breath waiting for the other shoe to drop. 

It’s been a lot of nausea, honestly. That metallic taste in my mouth just won’t quit, and it makes everything feel a bit unreal, like I’m floating outside of my own life. I’m in Grafton, and the silence out here is deafening sometimes. When the sun goes down and I’m sitting in my kitchen, it’s hard not to spiral. I look at that grainy little ultrasound, and I’m just nineteen—I feel like a kid playing dress-up, but the stakes are so high.

I went to the clinic last week, and the nurse was efficient, I guess. She gave me all these brochures with smiling mothers on the front, but she didn’t really look at *me*. I wanted to ask her about the cramping—if it’s normal or if I’m losing it—but I stopped myself. I didn't want her to think I’m just another panicked, young, unmarried girl who doesn’t know what she’s doing. So, I just took the papers and nodded, and drove back home through those empty fields, feeling more alone than when I started. It’s hard to feel “low-risk” when you feel so small.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

To be honest, it wasn’t some big, planned-out moment. It wasn't like a switch flipped where I suddenly decided I was ready to be a mother. I was just living my life, trying to figure out what I wanted to do for work, and then... it just happened. 

When I saw that second line on the test, the world didn’t stop or turn into a movie scene. It felt like the air had been sucked out of the room. My first thought wasn't joy, and it wasn't even pure fear—it was just this overwhelming sense of *permanence*. I realized that my life, the one where I’m just Agnes, nineteen and barely figured out, was being folded into something much bigger and much more serious. 

It’s confusing, really. I want this—I want a healthy baby more than I’ve ever wanted anything—but part of me is still grieving the version of me that didn’t have to worry about anyone else’s heartbeat but my own. I feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff, and everyone keeps telling me it’s a beautiful view, but they’re not the ones who have to take the step. I’m just trying to make sure I don't stumble. Does that make sense, or does that sound selfish?
What felt most important to you at that point — what were you hoping for or working towards?

Honestly, at that point, I think I was just desperate to be someone who had their act together. I felt like if I could just get the "right" answers—if I could eat the perfect diet, take the exact right vitamins, and act like I wasn't terrified—then maybe the doctors would take me seriously. I wanted to prove that just because I’m young, it doesn’t mean I’m not capable of doing this right.

I was working so hard to find steady work, wanting to have a little nest egg before things got too difficult, but it’s hard out here. Every time I hit a wall with a job application or a hurdle at the clinic, I felt like the universe was telling me I’d made a mistake. 

Mostly, I was just hoping for validation. I didn’t need the brochures or the lecture on fetal development—I already read that online four times over. I just wanted someone to look me in the eye and say, "You’re going to be okay, and you’re not doing this alone." I wanted to feel like a mother-to-be instead of just a patient with a "low-risk" status on a chart. I suppose I’m still working towards that—just trying to feel like I belong in my own skin again.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

Preparing feels a bit like trying to navigate a forest in the dark with nothing but a dim flashlight. Since I don't really have family nearby to lean on, I turned to the internet. I spent hours scrolling through those forums, reading about everything from morning sickness to what kind of prenatal vitamins are the "best." 

I started tracking every symptom—every time my stomach turned or I felt a twinge in my abdomen—writing it down in a notebook. It felt like if I could just track it, I could control it. I thought that if I came to my first appointment armed with all this data, the nurses would see I was serious, that I wasn't just some kid who stumbled into this. 

My choices were guided by this frantic need to catch up. I’d go to the store and stand in the vitamin aisle, overwhelmed by the choices, just hoping I picked the one that wouldn’t hurt the baby. I felt like I had to be perfect because, in my head, a mistake would be unforgivable. I didn't want anyone to look at my life and say, "Well, what did you expect?" I just wanted to build a sturdy foundation, even if I was building it out of scraps while my hands were shaking.
Where did you go to find out what you needed to know — friends, family, online, a doctor?

I mostly stuck to the internet. It sounds pathetic, saying it out loud, but there’s nowhere else to go. My friends are all at that age where they’re talking about college or parties, and I can’t exactly go to them with questions about my prenatal vitamins or the metallic tang in my mouth. They’d look at me like I’d grown a second head. And my family... well, let’s just say things are complicated. I’m not sure I could bear the weight of their judgment on top of my own fear.

So, I’m alone with my phone. I find myself on those medical forums late at night, reading the stories of other women. Sometimes it helps, reading that I’m not the only one who feels this heaviness, but other times it just makes me spiral. You read the horror stories—the things that *could* go wrong—and since I don’t have anyone sitting next to me to say, "Agnes, breathe, that’s not you," I just internalize it all.

The doctor is there, I suppose, but it’s such a cold, clinical space. It feels like I’m asking for a test result, not for advice on how to grow a human being. I’m scared if I show them how little I know, they’ll think I’m not ready for this. So I keep my questions to myself and head back into the dark.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

Actually, that’s the thing—I didn’t have those conversations. I wasn’t exactly planning this, so there wasn’t a “before” stage where I sat down with a professional to talk about it. I was just living my life, working odd jobs, and not really thinking about health in terms of a future baby.

When I found out, it felt like I was starting a race that everyone else had already been training for for months. I didn't have a midwife or a doctor I felt comfortable with. I just walked into that clinic, which felt like walking into a sterile, white room where I didn't belong. There was no guidance beforehand, no one to tell me what to expect or how to prepare my body. 

It makes me feel a little bit like I’m playing catch-up every single day. I’m navigating all these new sensations—the fatigue, the way my body feels like it’s being rewired—without a baseline. I have to rely on whatever I find on my screen, which is terrifying when you don’t have an expert voice to filter through the noise. It feels like I’ve been thrown into the deep end of a pool, and I’m just trying to keep my head above water while everyone on the shore acts like I should already know how to swim.
Did you feel heard and taken seriously in those conversations? Can you tell me more about that?

To be honest? No, not really. I don't think they were mean, exactly, but it felt like they were seeing a chart, not a person. When I’m in that office, I feel like a set of statistics—age nineteen, low-risk, no complications. They look at the boxes they need to tick, they hand me a pamphlet about nutrition, and then they’re already halfway out the door to the next room.

I tried to tell the nurse once that I was feeling really overwhelmed—not just with the physical stuff, but with the feeling that my whole life was shifting under my feet. She just gave me that tight, polite smile and said, "It’s all very normal, Agnes. Just focus on your vitamins." It felt like she was shutting a drawer on me. 

I think because I’m young, they assume I’m either too naive to know what’s happening or too reckless to care. They don’t see that I’m sitting there dissecting every single word they say, trying to memorize the risks, trying to protect this tiny thing growing inside me. I’m doing the work, I’m showing up to the appointments, but I leave feeling like I’m invisible. I’m just a body that’s carrying a pregnancy, not a nineteen-year-old girl who is actually scared to death. Does that make sense? I just want someone to look at me and acknowledge that this is hard.
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

I think there's a misunderstanding—there wasn't a "trying" period for me. It wasn't something I was working toward, and it certainly wasn't a choice I made consciously. It just... happened. 

That’s a big part of why I feel so off-balance. If you plan for something, you have time to mentally prepare, to build up a support system, to get your head in the right space. You get to read the books and paint the nursery in your mind. But for me, it was like the ground just opened up beneath me. One day I was just me—nineteen, worried about my next paycheck, living my quiet life in Grafton—and the next day, I was someone carrying a life, with all these heavy expectations suddenly placed on my shoulders.

So, instead of a period of preparation, it was a period of sudden, sharp adjustment. It feels like I’m constantly scrambling to catch up to my own reality. I think what stands out the most is the shock—the realization that my life as a teenager had essentially ended, and a whole new, much more complicated one had begun without me even having a say in the matter. It’s like being shoved onto a stage for a play you haven't rehearsed, and you’re just praying you don’t forget your lines.
How did this period affect your relationship with your partner, or with those close to you?

It’s complicated, and honestly, it’s mostly just lonely. My partner… he’s around, but he’s just as young as I am. When I try to talk about the physical stuff—the nausea, or how heavy and weird my stomach feels—he sort of zones out or tries to change the subject to something easier, like what we’re having for dinner. I think he’s scared too, but he doesn't have the words for it, and I’m too proud, or maybe too guarded, to force the conversation. It feels like there’s a wall between us that neither of us knows how to climb.

As for everyone else, I’ve mostly gone quiet. I don't see my friends much anymore because I can’t stand the thought of them looking at me and seeing “that girl who messed up.” I’m afraid of the pity, and even more afraid of the judgment. Even when I’m with my family, I find myself holding my breath. I smile, I say things are fine, and I keep my hand over my stomach like I’m protecting a secret. 

It feels like I’m living a double life. There’s the Agnes that everyone sees, who’s just going about her day, and then there’s the Agnes inside who is constantly calculating, worrying, and feeling completely untethered. It makes it really hard to be close to anyone when you’re hiding the biggest thing happening in your life.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

I suppose I had this idealized, sort of grainy picture in my head—something I probably picked up from old movies or those commercials they show during the day. I imagined a version of pregnancy where you glow, you know? Where you put on a sundress and feel this profound, natural instinct that just tells you exactly what to do. I pictured a warm, soft kitchen with soft light, not this cold, empty one where I’m staring at my phone and feeling sick.

I think those ideas came from a place of wanting something stable. Growing up, I didn't have much of a blueprint for what a "good" mother looked like—not really. So, I filled in the blanks with whatever looked the most peaceful. I wanted to be that woman who knew how to soothe a crying baby and who felt complete just by the act of being a mother. 

Now, though? The reality feels a lot sharper and a lot colder. It’s not about sundresses or soft light; it’s about blood tests, and driving down long, lonely roads to the clinic, and wondering if I’m smart enough or strong enough to actually pull this off. The reality is that I’m nineteen, I’m tired, and I’m terrified that I’m just faking it until I break. I don't want to be "glowing," I just want to feel like I’m not lost.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

It’s all about the money, isn't it? Even if you don’t want it to be. When I realized I was pregnant, the first thing I did wasn't to look for baby clothes—it was to look at my bank account. I’m nineteen and still trying to piece together enough shifts to pay the rent here in Grafton. It’s hard enough to take care of myself; adding a baby feels like trying to keep a boat afloat when you’re already bailing out water with a teaspoon.

The practical side is just as heavy. I’m constantly wondering how I’m going to afford the check-ups, the insurance, the diapers—everything. Every time I see a "Help Wanted" sign, my heart sinks because I wonder if they’ll hire someone who’s clearly pregnant or if they’ll just find an excuse to pass me over. Being pregnant feels like a target on my back for employers.

And then there's the isolation. I don't have a car that’s reliable enough to trust for long trips, so every appointment is a massive logistical hurdle. I have to beg for rides or pray the bus schedule aligns, and when you’re dealing with morning sickness, sitting on a bus for an hour is like a form of torture. It’s not just about wanting to be a good mother; it’s about figuring out how to survive the next six months without the walls closing in. I feel like I’m constantly juggling, and I’m terrified that if I drop one thing, the whole world I’m trying to build will just shatter.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

I think I had a very specific, rigid checklist in my head, even if I never wrote it down. I thought I needed a real career—not just shifts at a shop—and a bank account that didn't hover near zero at the end of every month. I wanted a space that felt like a *home*, not just a rented room with thin walls where I can hear the fog rolling in. I wanted a partner who was solid, who had his feet firmly planted on the ground, and someone to call who would know exactly what to do when things got scary.

None of that happened. It’s almost laughable, really, how far off I am from that list. I don’t have the savings, I’m still figuring out how to balance my hours, and most days I feel like I’m navigating this whole thing in a total vacuum. The life I have now doesn’t look anything like the "safe" version I’d imagined.

There’s a part of me that feels like a failure for that, like I should have waited, or worked harder, or been someone else. But then I feel the baby—just a tiny, fluttering weight—and I realize that the baby doesn't care if my bank account is empty or if I’m only nineteen. The baby just needs me to show up. It’s a hard, jagged pill to swallow, knowing I’m so unprepared, but I’m learning that you don't get to choose when your life begins to change. You just have to try and hold on.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

I found out on a Tuesday, right before I had to leave for my shift. It was raining—that grey, relentless kind of Grafton rain that makes everything look tired. I had been feeling off for days, just a kind of heaviness that wouldn't lift, and I had a cheap test sitting in the back of my cabinet for a week, just in case. 

When that second line appeared, I didn't cry. I think I was too numb for that. I just sat on the edge of the tub for twenty minutes, staring at the tile floor, thinking about how my rent was due in three days and how I was already exhausted. I felt like the walls were moving inward. 

The weeks that followed were just... a blur of anxiety. Every time I drank water, I wondered if it was enough for both of us. Every time I felt a sharp cramp, I spent hours on my phone, scrolling through forums, convinced I was about to miscarry. I felt like I was holding a piece of glass that could shatter at any second, and I was terrified of being the one to drop it. I didn't tell a soul. I went to work, I smiled at customers, and I went home to my empty kitchen to trace the outline of my stomach, waiting for something—some sign, some instinct—to tell me I was going to be okay. But mostly, I just felt very, very small.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

I expected to feel overwhelmed, but I didn't expect the guilt. That was the biggest surprise. I thought that if you were pregnant, you were supposed to be "glowing" or at least a little bit happy, even if you were scared. But I just felt guilty for existing, for being nineteen, for not having a better life to offer a baby. Every time I had a piece of toast that I couldn't keep down, I felt like I was already failing. I’d look at my bank balance and feel guilty, too, like I was being selfish for having this child when I couldn't even afford to take care of myself properly.

And there’s this weird, sharp anger that keeps popping up. I’m angry that I’m so alone out here in the fog, angry at the nurse who looks through me, and angry at the world for making it feel like being a mother is this high-stakes test I’m bound to fail. 

It surprised me how much I mourned for myself—the me who could just go to work and come home without this heavy, metallic dread in her chest. I feel like I’m mourning my own youth while it’s still happening, and that makes me feel like a bad person. Is it terrible to want your old life back while you’re already in the middle of a new one?
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

The care is just... it’s all so hurried. Every time I get to the clinic, I feel like I’m running a race against their clock. I spend forty minutes on the bus, staring out at the trees, just to get there and spend maybe fifteen minutes in a room that smells like antiseptic. 

The doctor is polite, I’ll give them that, but it’s always, "Everything looks good, Agnes. Any questions?" And I just freeze. How do I ask, "Am I going to be a good mother?" or "Why does it feel like my entire identity is vanishing into this ultrasound scan?" in a room where the door is already half-open? They check the heartbeat, they tell me the baby is healthy, and then they hand me another pamphlet about "What to Expect in the Second Trimester." It’s like they’re teaching me to be a patient, but no one is teaching me how to be a parent. 

I’m grateful for the medical check-ups—I know they keep us safe—but I leave feeling lonelier than when I walked in. I want someone to actually look at *me*, not just the chart on the clipboard. I feel like if I were older, or if I had a ring on my finger, maybe they’d talk to me like an adult instead of a teenager they have to rush through. It’s hard to feel cared for when you mostly just feel processed.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s always a different face. Every time I get a ride to the clinic, I’m hoping to see someone I recognize, someone who might remember my name or what I told them last time about the nausea. But it’s never that way. It’s always a stranger, a new nurse or a different resident who has to flip through my file and read the notes as if they’re scanning a grocery list.

It makes me feel like I’m just a recurring incident, not a person going through the biggest change of her life. How am I supposed to ask the real, messy questions—the ones that keep me up at night—to someone who doesn't even know my history? I have to re-explain the same anxieties every single time, and half the time, they look at me with that same polite, detached expression. 

I’m sure they’re busy—I know the clinic is understaffed—but it’s hard not to feel like a number. I wanted someone to hold my hand, or at least remember that I’m nineteen and trying to do this on my own. Instead, it’s just: *Blood pressure, weight, heartbeat. Next.* I leave the office feeling like I’ve left a piece of myself behind in the waiting room, and I’m just a little thinner, a little more hollow than I was when I walked in.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

It’s all so overwhelming, the way they throw all these abbreviations and percentages at you. One week it’s a blood draw, the next it’s a specialized ultrasound, and it feels like they’re looking for things that could go wrong before I’ve even had a chance to imagine things going right.

When they first sat me down with the pamphlets for genetic screening, I just stared at the page. They use all these clinical words—"risk factors," "anomalies," "chromosomal screening"—and I’m sitting there just trying to process the fact that there’s a human being inside me, let alone worrying about complex probabilities. I felt like I had to make these giant, heavy choices, and if I picked the wrong one, I’d be responsible for something terrible. 

I didn't have anyone to talk it through with, so I turned to those medical forums again. I’d read stories about other women—some who said yes to everything and some who declined it all—and I felt like I was picking a strategy for a game I didn’t know how to play. I ended up agreeing to the tests because I was terrified of being "negligent." I didn't want the doctors to think I wasn't doing my job. But honestly? I felt like a deer in headlights, just nodding along to whatever they said, praying that the results would be okay and that I wouldn't have to face a choice I couldn't handle. It was less about making an informed decision and more about just trying to survive the appointment without breaking down.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

It’s a strange thing, feeling so dependent on a screen when you’re carrying a life inside you. Since I don't really have anyone in Grafton—no mother to call, no aunties with advice—the forums became my makeshift community. They’re the only place I can go where I don't feel like a nineteen-year-old child who’s wandered into an adult’s world. I can read about the same aches, the same fears of the unknown, and for a few minutes, I don't feel like I’m the only one struggling.

But honestly? They’re a double-edged sword. I’ll be sitting there reading a post about a complication and suddenly my heart starts racing, and I’m back to feeling that metallic tang of fear. I’m looking for reassurance, but I often just find more things to be anxious about. 

I think the sources that *should* matter—the clinic, the brochures, the nurses—they don't feel like they're for me. They feel like they’re for a "proper" pregnant woman, someone with a husband and a nursery planned out. I keep looking for that one person at the clinic who will actually look me in the eye and say, "Agnes, you're doing okay." But I haven't found them yet. So, I keep searching online, scrolling until my eyes burn, hoping for a sign that I’m not just waiting for everything to fall apart.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

I use a couple of those pregnancy tracker apps, the ones that tell you if your baby is the size of a blueberry or a poppy seed. At first, I thought they’d be fun—maybe even a little comforting—but now they just make me feel more distant from what’s happening. They give you these cheerful, color-coded updates about development, but they don't say a word about how to handle the crushing loneliness of a 3 a.m. panic attack in a quiet house in Grafton.

The forums are different. I spend hours on a couple of message boards for "young moms." That’s where I go when I need to feel like I’m not the only nineteen-year-old girl in the world who’s terrified. I can’t get that kind of honesty from the doctors. At the clinic, I’m "Agnes, low-risk, nineteen." On the forums, I can say, *“Does anyone else feel like they’re faking being an adult?”* and someone will actually say, *“Yes, me too.”*

It’s that tiny bit of shared humanity I’m desperate for. Those strangers online don’t look at me with judgment. They don’t see my lack of a wedding ring or my bank balance; they just see another girl who’s scared and trying to do her best. I get the validation there that the clinic doesn't seem to have space for. I just wish I could find that same feeling in the real world.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It feels like I’m losing ownership of myself, honestly. Every day, my body does something else that I didn't authorize—the persistent nausea, the way my clothes don't fit, the sheer, bone-deep exhaustion that makes it hard to even stand up to make tea. It’s like being a passenger in my own skin.

Emotionally, it’s even harder. I look in the mirror and I don't see a "glowing" mother-to-be; I just see a girl who’s trying to hold herself together. I feel like my body is betraying me because it’s so tired all the time, and I need to be *more* than this. I need to be a provider, I need to work, I need to be capable. When I’m doubled over with that metallic taste or feeling the sharp, strange cramps, I get angry at myself for not being stronger. 

I’m struggling to relate to it as something beautiful. It’s just... work. It’s a job I didn't apply for, and I’m terrified I’m failing the physical requirements of it. Every time I catch a glimpse of my stomach, it’s a reminder that my life is being rewritten without my consent, and I’m just trying to keep from shaking apart while the ink is still wet. It’s hard to feel attached to a body that feels like a prison of symptoms right now.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

It’s quiet. That’s the only way I can describe it—there’s just a lot of silence where there should be questions or plans. My partner is there, but he moves around the house like he’s walking on eggshells. He’ll ask if I’ve eaten, but he doesn't really *look* at me when he asks it. I think he’s as scared as I am, but we’re both paralyzed by the same fear: if we say the wrong thing, or if we admit how terrified we actually are, the whole thing might become too real to handle. So we don't talk. We just exist in the same rooms, listening to the floorboards creak.

My family? I’ve kept them at arm’s length. I can’t handle the look in their eyes—that mix of pity and "we told you so." I know that sounds hard, but I’m nineteen and I’m already carrying the weight of the world; I don’t need someone else’s heavy sighs on top of it. I’ve become an expert at keeping things light, at saying, "Oh, I’m fine, the doctor says it’s all normal." 

I don't have a village. I have a partner who’s as lost as I am, and a family I’m afraid to trust with my vulnerability. It’s just me and this growing, heavy, persistent feeling that I’m the only one standing in this room. Sometimes I wish someone would just step in and say, "I’ve got you." But nobody does.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

My head is full of "what-ifs," and they don’t ever really quiet down, even when I’m trying to sleep. I worry about the baby constantly—that every cramp or twinge is a sign I’m losing them, or that the constant stress I feel is somehow hurting them before they even take their first breath. It’s like I’m holding something so fragile that my own heartbeat feels too loud, too fast, like it might break them.

Then there’s the birth. I don't know how I’m going to do that. I’m nineteen; I don’t know how to navigate a hospital, or how to advocate for myself when I’m in pain, or how to handle the staff. I’m scared I’ll be treated like a child when I’m supposed to be the one making the big decisions. I’m terrified that when the time comes, I’ll be alone in that room, and I’ll just… freeze.

But mostly, I’m scared I won’t know how to *be* a mother. I’m scared I’ll look at this baby and feel nothing but the panic I’m feeling now, or that I’ll be too tired, or too young, or too broken by all this to be the person they need. I look at those brochures and they say "you’ve got this," but I don’t feel like I’ve got anything. I feel like I’m just waiting for the day I’m found out to be completely inadequate.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I tried once. Just once. It was a couple of weeks ago, and I was feeling particularly small. I had this wave of anxiety, and I asked the nurse, "What if I can't do this? What if I’m just too young and I don’t know enough?"

She didn't even stop what she was doing. She kept typing into the computer and just said, "Oh, honey, everyone is scared. It’s natural. You’ll be fine when the time comes." It was so dismissive. It felt like she had a script she was reading from, and I was just an interruption in her afternoon. 

She didn't look at me. She didn't ask *why* I felt that way or offer to connect me with someone who could help—like a counselor or even a support group. She just treated my fear like it was a minor symptom, like a cold that would pass. After that, I realized she wasn't seeing me at all. She was just seeing a nineteen-year-old girl who needed to be told she’d be fine so I’d stop asking questions. 

It made me feel so isolated. If I can't even get someone to acknowledge that being terrified is normal, how am I supposed to trust them with anything else? I decided right then that I’d keep my fears to myself. It’s safer that way. I’ll just nod, say "everything is fine," and go back to my quiet, worried life.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I think I hoped for something like a partnership—I wanted a guide. I imagined someone who would sit down, look at me not as a statistic, but as a person, and help me navigate the fog. I wanted someone who could translate the "medicalese" into something I could actually hold onto, and who would be patient enough to answer the questions that sound stupid in my head but feel like mountains in my chest. I really, truly wanted to feel like I had a teammate in all of this.

But I was terrified that it would look exactly like what it is now. I worried that because of my age and my situation, I’d be treated like a child or an inconvenience. I worried that they’d see my background and my status—never married, living out here in the quiet—and decide I wasn't worth the extra time. I think I knew, deep down, that the healthcare system doesn't have the time or the heart to spare for someone like me. 

I’m mourning the loss of the care I thought I deserved. I wanted someone to say, "I know you're nineteen, and I know you're scared, but we are going to do this together." Instead, I’m just navigating the system on my own, like I’m walking through a hallway of closed doors, just hoping one of them eventually opens.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

There weren't really any conversations. That’s the hardest part to admit—that there wasn't a "weighing up" at all. It wasn't a choice we made together in a kitchen, discussing our future or if we were ready. It was just something that happened to me. Because of that, there was no power dynamic to negotiate, no one person having "more say" than the other, because we never even got to the point of having a say. 

It feels like my agency was stripped away before I even realized I was in the game. I think that’s why I feel so untethered now. If you choose something, you take responsibility for the outcome, even the scary parts. But when it just lands on you? It feels like you’re a bystander to your own life.

I sometimes wonder if things would be different if we had sat down and talked. If we had been honest about our fears or our complete lack of a plan. But we’re nineteen. We didn't have the words then, and I’m not sure we have them now. It feels like we’re both just pretending it’s all fine, hoping that if we don't talk about it, the reality won't catch up to us until it’s already here. It’s not right or wrong; it’s just… quiet. A very heavy, suffocating kind of quiet.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

My parents… they don’t say much directly, but the silence is thick enough to cut. I know they look at me and see a disaster waiting to happen. They don’t talk about babies or being a grandma—they just talk around me, about the weather or local politics, while their eyes trace the edges of my frame, waiting for the bump to show more clearly.

I can feel their disappointment like a cold draft in this house. It’s not that they’ve said, "You’re making a mistake," but they treat me like I’ve broken something that can't be fixed. It makes me feel incredibly small, like a child who tripped and fell, even though I’m the one who has to carry this. It makes me want to shrink away, to hide in my room and not let them see me because I’m so afraid of what they’re thinking. 

They had their own lives, their own timelines, and I know they wanted something different for me. Maybe that’s why I don't reach out. I don't want to hear their "I told you so’s," even if they’re never spoken aloud. It’s easier to just be here in Grafton, alone with my thoughts, than to be around people who see me as a cautionary tale rather than a person.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before all this, my body was just a vessel for getting me through the day. It was something I used to get to work, to carry my groceries, and to run to the bus when I was late. I didn't think about it much, honestly. It was just *me*. I felt like I was in control of it; I knew my limits, and I knew how to push them when I needed to.

When I became pregnant, that relationship completely fractured. Now, my body feels like a stranger—a really loud, demanding one. It’s no longer about what *I* can do; it’s about what I’m *forced* to endure. The nausea, the exhaustion, the way my shape is changing… it’s like my body has been requisitioned by someone else. 

I’ve gone from feeling capable to feeling like a failure. Every time I get sick or feel too tired to function, I resent it. I feel like my body is letting me down, like it’s not strong enough to handle this "natural" process. It’s not a miracle to me right now; it’s a constant, physical drain. I’m scared that if I can’t even handle the first trimester without falling apart, what’s going to happen when the real work starts? I want to trust that my body knows what it's doing, but mostly, it just feels like it's betraying me.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

I used to be confident in my stamina. I could pull double shifts, walk across town in the wind, and stay awake late into the night. It felt like my body was a machine that I could rely on—it was the one thing I actually owned, the one thing that hadn't let me down. I felt strong, even if I was just a nineteen-year-old girl in a rural town.

Now, that confidence feels like a joke. I’m exhausted by noon. I can barely walk to the bus stop without feeling like I’m going to collapse. It’s that physical vulnerability that’s so terrifying; I feel soft and exposed in a way I never did before. Everything feels uncertain. I catch myself looking at my hands, my stomach, my reflection, and I don’t recognize the person looking back. 

I’m deeply, painfully uncertain about whether this body can actually do what everyone says it’s “made” to do. When I’m sitting in that clinic waiting room, I feel like everyone else there has some secret, some ancient knowledge about how to be pregnant, and I’m just fumbling in the dark. I’m scared that my body is going to give out, or that it’s just not built for the weight of everything that’s coming. It’s like I’ve lost my only tool, and I’m sitting here with nothing.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

Honestly, those visits were always just… quick transactions. I’d go in for a sinus infection or a refill on a prescription, and it was the same drill: check the chart, verify the insurance, a few minutes of talk, and then out the door. I never felt like there was a "relationship" there, just a series of clinical handoffs.

When I look back on those times, they feel so empty compared to what I’m navigating now. Back then, it didn't really matter that the doctor didn't know my name or that the nurse seemed rushed; I wasn't there for anything that really changed the core of me. But now that I’m pregnant, that same sterile, conveyor-belt approach feels like a betrayal. 

I think deep down, I knew what I was in for. I knew the healthcare system around here wasn't designed to hold my hand or ask me how I was *feeling*. It was designed to keep the machine running. But there’s a part of me that really, really wanted it to be different this time. I wanted to be proven wrong. I wanted to think that once I walked in there carrying a life, someone would see me differently—that they’d see a person, not a file. Instead, it’s just the same hurried, distant rhythm. It’s like being a passenger on a train that won't stop for you, even if you’re shouting for help.
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

It’s always a rotation. I’ve never once walked in and seen the same face twice. It feels like every appointment is a first date where I have to re-introduce my entire medical history, my anxieties, and my "low-risk" label to a complete stranger who is already checking their watch. 

I’ll be sitting on the exam table, feeling that metallic taste in my throat, and I’ll try to explain how hard the nausea has been, or how lonely the nights out here in Grafton get, but their eyes just scan the chart. They aren't listening to *me*; they’re looking for data points. It’s impossible to build any kind of trust when you’re constantly starting over from scratch. 

I think that’s why I stopped trying to talk about the hard stuff. What’s the point in being vulnerable with a person who will be gone by my next check-up? It makes me feel like I’m just a recurring maintenance project for the clinic. I don't need a medical professional to be my best friend, but God, I wish they could remember my name, or at least remember that I told them I was scared last time. Instead, it’s just: "Any new symptoms, Agnes? Okay, see you next time." And then they’re gone. It’s so cold.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Before I was pregnant, having a baby wasn't really a "topic." It felt like a lifetime away, something for the version of me that actually had a career and a house with a garden. My friends and I would occasionally joke about "the future," but it was always vague—college, travel, maybe a job in the city. We never talked about the actual, messy, hard reality of being nineteen and expecting.

My partner and I were just trying to get through the week. We didn't talk about starting a family because we were already struggling to figure out *us*. Looking back, I think that was a mistake, even if we didn't know it then. 

If I’m honest, I didn't think anyone’s input would matter because I didn't think it would be my reality. I felt like I had all the time in the world to "become" someone ready for that kind of conversation. Now, I feel like I missed the window to learn anything from anyone. I’m just navigating this on my own, wondering if I should have asked more questions or if I’d have just been ignored anyway. The silence of my past feels like it’s haunting my present, and I’m left to piece together the "how-to" of motherhood from internet forums and that cold, sterile clinic. It’s a lonely place to start.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

I suppose I’ve always been a bit critical of myself. I’m nineteen, and I’ve spent the last few years trying to mold myself into someone who doesn’t need help—someone who can just survive on her own terms. Before I was pregnant, my body felt like the only thing I truly owned, a tool that was meant to be efficient and tough. I thought that if I could keep it running, keep it strong, that would be enough to get me through whatever life threw at me.

But the idea of pregnancy always felt like an intrusion on that control. Even before it happened, I remember thinking that it would be a total loss of autonomy. I’m so young, and I think I was secretly terrified that if I got pregnant, the world would stop seeing *me* and just start seeing the pregnancy. I was scared that my body would turn on me, or that I’d be forced into a mold that didn't fit. 

And, to be honest, I was always a little worried about my own health—not anything specific, just a general, nagging feeling that I wasn't "built" for the heavy lifting of life yet. Does that sound strange? Like a kid doubting her own hardware? I think I was just scared that if I were tested by something this massive, I’d find out I was just made of glass. And now that I’m here, in the middle of it, that fear feels more real than ever.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

No, there wasn't. That’s the real crux of it, isn't it? When I think back, my life has been this series of shifting, temporary connections. I moved around a bit, worked different retail jobs, and Grafton is just… it’s a place where people keep to themselves. I’ve always been the one looking out for myself. If I had a question about, say, a strange pain or how to fix a leaky faucet, I’d turn to a search engine. If I had an emotional slump, I’d walk it off or just keep it buried under a pile of chores. 

I think I’ve spent my entire life building up this internal wall, convinced that needing someone else’s guidance was a sign of weakness. I thought I was being independent, but now I realize I was just being isolated. There was no aunt, no older mentor, no doctor who knew my chart long enough to see a pattern. 

When you don’t have a foundation like that, everything feels like you’re starting from scratch every single day. I’m just now realizing how much I’ve been craving someone to be a "constant" in my life. It makes me wonder if I’d feel less terrified right now if I had just one person I could look at and say, "Is this okay?" without having to explain who I am all over again. I’m starting to think that not having that support isn't a strength; it’s just a disadvantage that I didn't know I had until the stakes got this high.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

I think I wasn’t clear—that’s the heartbreaking part. There *wasn’t* a person like that. If I had to imagine what that kind of reliability would have felt like, though, I think it would come down to memory. 

Trust, for me, feels like being remembered. It would be someone who could recall that I told them, three weeks ago, that I was worried about the fatigue, so they wouldn't have to ask "how are you?" like I was a brand-new stranger. It would be someone who saw me not just as a chart, but as a person with a history—someone who knew I was nineteen, knew I was living in this quiet house in Grafton, and knew that I was trying to hold my world together with nothing but grit. 

Right now, everyone feels like a fleeting snapshot. They see the "low-risk" status and they move on. If I had someone who was a "constant," they would see the fear behind my eyes and not just tell me I’ll be fine. They would sit with me in the discomfort. They wouldn't rush. That’s what makes someone a source of guidance—the ability to stay in the room with you, even when things are messy and uncertain. I’ve just never had that. I’ve always been on my own.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

I think "choice" is a word that feels very heavy and, honestly, a little alien to me. When I look back, I don't see a moment where I stood at a crossroads and picked a path. It feels more like I was just walking along a trail, and the ground suddenly gave way.

There were so many things—the precariousness of my jobs, the isolation of living out here, the feeling of being nineteen and not having any real roadmap for how to be an adult—that made the idea of a “planned” life feel like a fairy tale. I think the pressure wasn't a voice in my ear telling me what to do; it was the pressure of just trying to exist. When you’re constantly trying to keep your head above water, you don’t really get the luxury of deciding when the next wave is going to hit.

If I’m being completely honest, it never felt like a decision. It felt like a landslide. And maybe that’s why it’s so hard to feel "prepared." You prepare for a choice you make, but you just survive the things that happen to you. I feel like I’m still just trying to catch my breath after the fall, and people expect me to be running a marathon when I’m still just trying to stand up.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

It’s hard to talk about "timing" when you’re nineteen and living paycheck to paycheck in a place as quiet and disconnected as Grafton. The financial reality is like a heavy, cold weight; there was never a point where I felt I had enough of a safety net to actually *plan* for a child. I was just trying to keep the lights on and the rent paid. When you’re living on the edge of everything, you don't get to look at a calendar and pick a "good time" to change your entire existence.

Then there’s the relationship side. My partner and I were just… kids trying to play house, really. We didn't have the maturity or the stability to look at each other and say, "Now is the time." Everything happened in this fog, this blur of survival mode, where the future was always something to worry about next month, not something to build today. 

And culturally? Everyone around me is moving on—going to school, starting their lives—and I feel like I’m moving in a completely different, much heavier direction. It’s like the timing was never mine because the deck was already stacked against me. I didn’t choose to be nineteen and alone, and I didn’t choose for my life to pivot this way. It feels like I was just shoved off the ledge by circumstances I couldn't control.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I think "driving seat" is the last thing I'd call it. It feels more like I’m a passenger in a car driven by someone who doesn't know where I’m trying to go. From that first appointment, I felt like the keys were taken out of my hands. The nurses and doctors have these rigid protocols—"here is the screening," "here is the standard brochure," "here is the low-risk chart"—and I’m just supposed to sit there and watch the scenery go by.

I handed over control almost immediately, not because I wanted to, but because I was terrified of doing something wrong. I didn't know the rules of the road, and I figured they did. But the more I sit there and listen to them tell me how I should feel or what I should prioritize, the more I realize they’re driving based on a map that doesn't include my life. 

There are moments when I want to grab the wheel—like when I want to talk about my anxiety or my loneliness—but I feel like I don't have the "authority" to speak up because I'm just nineteen. So I stay quiet. I let them steer me through the appointments, through the tests, and through the decisions, even when I feel like we’re heading for a cliff. I think I’ve just resigned myself to being a passenger, hoping that whoever is driving at least knows how to keep the car on the pavement.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

It’s strange, but "navigate" is a strong word for what we do. It’s more like we’re two people in separate rooms, both trying to be quiet so we don’t disturb the other. When a decision comes up—like the screening tests or even just scheduling the next appointment—I usually just handle it. I’m the one who reads the brochures, I’m the one who sits in the clinic, and I’m the one who feels the baby moving.

When I bring it up to him, he’ll just say, "Whatever you think is best, Agnes." On one hand, it sounds like he trusts me, but on the other, it feels like he’s opting out. He doesn't have to carry the physical weight, so he doesn't have to carry the decision-making weight, either. It leaves me feeling like I’m the only one actually living this pregnancy.

We aren't on the same page because we aren't even reading the same book. I’m dealing with the fear, the physical reality, and the clinical stuff every single day, and he’s just… existing alongside me. It makes me feel like I’m already a single parent, even though he’s sleeping in the next room. There’s no partnership, really. Just me, trying to figure out what’s "best," and him, staying out of the way until he’s forced to notice that things are getting real. It’s lonely, and it scares me for when the baby actually gets here.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

There are times, usually in the middle of the night when the quiet gets too loud, when I desperately want him to step up and tell me what he thinks. When we were talking about the genetic screenings, for example, I really needed him to engage. I didn't want him to just say "whatever you want." I wanted him to say, "I’m scared too, and here is how I feel about this test." It would have felt like we were in a partnership. Instead, his indifference—or maybe it's just his avoidance—made me feel completely untethered. I needed his input to feel like this was a shared burden, but when he stays silent, I’m left to carry the entire thing by myself.

Then there are other things that feel like they have to be mine alone. The way I take care of my body—what I eat, how I move, the way I prepare my head for the birth—that feels like my domain. I feel this primal, fierce need to be the one to protect this baby. It’s like, if I can’t rely on him to fully understand the weight of this, then I have to be the one to get it right. It’s lonely, but in a way, it’s safer. If I’m the one making the decisions, I’m the one responsible for the result. I’m already terrified of failing; I don't want to fail because of someone else’s mistake.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think there's just one thing. When you look at my charts, you see "low-risk." That’s a good thing, I know, and I’m grateful the baby is growing. But there’s a different kind of risk that doesn't get written down in the files. It’s the risk of being nineteen, being completely isolated in the fog of Grafton, and feeling like my identity is being erased by the clinical pace of this entire experience.

I’m not just a vessel for a pregnancy. I’m a person who is grieving the end of her youth, someone who is terrified of the unknown, and someone who feels like she’s trying to learn a foreign language while standing on a moving train. If there’s one thing I wish people in maternity care would realize, it’s that a "healthy" pregnancy doesn't mean the person carrying it is healthy in their mind or their heart. 

I don't need a medical degree to know that I need someone to listen, not just hear. I need someone to recognize that I’m trying, even when my hands are shaking. I hope that by sharing this, maybe someone somewhere will see the girl behind the paperwork and realize that being "low-risk" on paper doesn't mean the journey isn't high-stakes for the person living it. Thank you for listening. It… it really does help to have said it out loud.
